resources.string: ActuA11y shows how to build accessible Android apps. Browse the topics below to explore different accessibility patterns. If you use TalkBack, switch access, or a keyboard, this app is designed to work well with all of them.
Content Descriptions topic. Learn how images and icon buttons describe themselves to TalkBack. Activates the Content Descriptions topic screen.
Images and icon buttons need descriptions so TalkBack can read them aloud. Swipe through this screen with TalkBack on to hear how each example works.
A smartphone icon representing a mobile device
Focus this image with TalkBack. TalkBack reads aloud the content description set in code.
This button has no visible label. TalkBack reads the content description from code so you know what the button does.
TalkBack skips this image entirely. In code, its content description is set to null, which removes it from the accessibility tree.

For meaningful images, pass a non-null string to the Image contentDescription parameter — TalkBack reads it when the image is focused. For decorative images, pass null; Compose removes the node from the accessibility tree entirely.

For icon-only buttons, set contentDescription on the Icon inside IconButton. This is the only name TalkBack has for that button.

Modifier.semantics with paneTitle announces the screen name when a pane appears. Without it, TalkBack gives no feedback when the user navigates to a new screen.

Modifier.semantics with heading() marks text as a section heading so TalkBack users can jump between sections using a swipe gesture.

See: androidx.compose.ui.semantics.SemanticsProperties
